CASE STUDY

Tasking
Platform

A dual-role workforce management platform
for daily time logging, project visibility, leave
planning, and billing oversight.

B2B · SAAS INTERNAL PLATFORM REACT + FASTAPI
2 USER ROLES
1 SOLE DESIGNER
8+ CORE MODULES
Overview

One platform,
two realities

As the company grew past 20 people, managing timesheets in spreadsheets, billing in email threads, and tracking tasks via chat stopped working. Information was scattered, managers had no reliable visibility, and employees spent time on avoidable administrative overhead.

I was brought in as the sole designer and also the frontend developer to build an internal platform from scratch. The result is a React + FastAPI application with a dual-role system: a focused employee portal for daily work logging, and a management suite for oversight, billing, and reporting.

👤
Employee Role
Time portal, time reports, leave requests, notifications, and personal profile. Designed for speed: logging a full workday should take under 2 minutes.
📊
Management Role
Dashboard, project management, resource allocation, timesheets & leave, billing & licensing, reports, and settings. Built for decisions, not task-switching.
ReactFastAPITypeScriptTailwind CSSPythonSole Designer
Problem

The cost of
invisible work

Before this platform, the company ran on a patchwork: Excel timesheets emailed weekly, WhatsApp for task updates, and manually kept billing records in shared documents. The system worked at 5 people. It broke quietly at 20.

Core Design Challenge

How do you build a single platform that serves two radically different mental models an employee who just wants to log hours and leave, and a manager who needs a complete operational picture without making either feel like they're using the wrong tool?

3h
Lost per employee monthly
Time spent on timesheet reconciliation, resending files, and clarifying discrepancies across fragmented tools.
0
Real-time visibility
Managers had no way to see who was working on what, or how project hours tracked against estimates, without asking.
4+
Tools replaced
Excel sheets, email chains, WhatsApp groups, and a billing spreadsheet consolidated into one authenticated platform.
Design Process

From chaos
to system

I started by mapping every existing workflow through stakeholder interviews with both management and 6 employees who would become primary users. This surfaced two distinct mental models that had to coexist without collision.

The architecture decision came early: a single authenticated shell with role-based navigation. The sidebar and available screens are determined at login each user sees only what's relevant.

User Flow Dual-Role Architecture

Employee Portal
Ore · Raport · Concediu

Management Suite

Screen 01 — Angajat

Portal Ore — the 2-minute timesheet

The daily timesheet. Tasks are logged inline, each tagged with a colored project label. The Program Zilnic card above captures start time, break, and calculates total hours automatically. The norma sidebar on the right tracks monthly progress without ever leaving the screen.

Time Portal — Portal ore
Screen 02 — Angajat

Raport Ore — monthly view

The employee's monthly report. Days are shown as cards — each card color-codes completion status (green = full hours, amber = partial, red = missing). Selecting a day expands its task list below. The sidebar tracks norma and project breakdown, giving employees a clear picture of where their hours went.

Raport Ore — monthly view: Luna tab
Screen 03 — Management

Project Management — all projects at a glance

The management project list. Every project row shows the colored badge, client, team members (overlapping avatars), budget vs. hours used, completion progress bar, and status. The filter bar above lets managers cut by client, team, or status. From here, any project opens into its own Gantt and sprint view.

Project Management — active projects list
Screen 04 — Management

Gantt — weekly planning

The project Gantt chart tracks stories and tasks across weekly columns. Each row shows hour chips — estimated (dashed border, italic) alongside actual (solid, colored by load: green = light, amber = moderate, red = over). Project rows sit on navy, module rows on light blue. The sticky task column keeps context visible while scrolling horizontally.

Gantt — weekly planning: Gantt proiecte tab
Screen 05 — Management

Timesheets — calendarul echipei

The management timesheet grid. Each row is an employee; each column is a day of the selected month. Cells are color-coded: green = hours submitted, amber = partial, red = missing, grey = weekend. Totals column on the right shows the month aggregate per person. One view replaces five email threads.

Timesheets — calendarul echipei: Calendar concedii tab
Screen 06 — Management

Billing & Licensing — contracts and licenses

License management for all clients in one table. Status badges, plan tiers, renewal dates with color-coded warnings for upcoming expirations. KPI cards give the summary at a glance: active, trial, suspended, and monthly revenue. Suspended rows are visually dimmed so the at-risk accounts stand out immediately.

Billing & Licensing — tab Licenses active
Design Decisions

Why it works
the way it does

Six decisions that shaped the platform — each one came from a real constraint, a research finding, or a moment where the obvious choice was wrong.

Decizie 01

Un shell, doua lumi: roluri, nu aplicatii separate

The first temptation was building two separate apps. It felt cleaner on paper: one codebase for employees, one for management. But this would have doubled authentication logic, fragmented deployment, and introduced a confusing onboarding question — "which app do I use?"

The solution: one authenticated shell where the sidebar items, routes, and available screens are determined by the role stored in the session token at login. Employees never see a Management section. Managers see both. Same URL, same login — entirely different experience.

tasking
platform
Bine ai venit!

Parola
••••••••
sau conecteaza-te rapid
...gantt view
Hours / week legend
10i8e
Estimat vs Efectuat
Celula dubla: estimat deasupra (italic, linie punctata), efectuat dedesubt (solid).
5e9e14e
Incarcatura colorata
Verde = normal. Galben = atentie (aproape de estimat). Rosu = depasit.
Why weekly, not daily? Software project estimates are imprecise at day level. A weekly view allows flexibility without losing control. A bug fix taking 3 days instead of 1 can be normal; if it exceeds the week, it becomes a real signal.
Decizie 02

Gantt pe saptamani: ore duble (estimat + efectuat)

Most Gantt tools show task bars across days. I designed this one with weekly columns and double hour chips: estimated (italic, dashed border) stacked above actual (solid, color-coded). This was the most contested design decision in the project.

The argument for daily: more granular. The argument against: software estimates are never accurate at the day level. Weekly granularity reduced noise while keeping budget deviation visible within one sprint. Green = on track. Amber = approaching limit. Red = over.

Decizie 03

Carduri zi in Raport Ore: culoarea e completarea

The weekly raport view shows 7 day cards (5 + weekend). Each card uses color as the primary signal: green = full hours, amber = partial, red = zero, grey = weekend. No table, no scan — employees understand their status in one glance.

This replaced a previous design with a simple list of dates. Users in testing missed the missing days because there was no visual alarm. Color-as-status reduced the average time to notice and fix a missing day from "days later" to "same session."

Decizie 04

Timesheet grid: colored cells replace text-only statuses

Management needed a monthly overview of all employee timesheets. The initial proposal was a table with status columns. During testing, managers skipped over text status labels and asked "who's missing days?" in every session.

The fix: a calendar-grid view where color IS the status. Green cells don't need reading. Red cells are impossible to miss. Managers can scan an entire month for 7 employees in seconds without reading a single number.

Decizie 05

Project badges: consistent visual identity across all screens

Project names in this company are long and follow the format "CLIENT | Descriere" — often several on the same client. In any dense list or table, users need to distinguish projects before reading the full name.

Each project gets a unique color and a 2-letter initial badge at creation. That color appears consistently: in the dashboard project list, in the portal ore task tags, in the Gantt row headers, and in the billing table. The color becomes the project's fingerprint across the entire product.

Decizie 06

Angajatii se vad doar pe ei: confidentialitate ca design decision

Early testing revealed an unexpected behavior: when employees could see their colleagues' hours, they started adjusting their own entries based on comparison — not based on actual time worked. It introduced social pressure into a logging tool.

The fix: employees see only their own norma, their own task history, and their own monthly totals. The "top angajati" comparison view exists only in the management dashboard. This wasn't a technical constraint — it was a deliberate trust decision.

Impact

Measurable
change

After launching internally, adoption was near-immediate. Within two weeks, all employees were logging timesheets through the platform a dramatic shift from the ~60% weekly completion rate with Excel.

98%
Timesheet completion rate
Up from ~60% with the previous Excel-based system
4h
Saved per manager weekly
Time previously spent on manual aggregation and license tracking
4→1
Tools replaced
Excel, WhatsApp, shared docs, and billing spreadsheet unified into one platform
Reflections

What I
learned

  • 01
    Being the sole designer changed my design decisions. Knowing I would implement every pixel made me ruthless about complexity. Owning the whole process meant every decision had to justify itself end-to-end.
  • 02
    Adoption is a design problem, not a communication problem. We could have trained employees on a complex interface and sent reminder emails. Instead, we made the timesheet feel like something they already knew. The 98% completion rate wasn't marketing it was the product working correctly.
  • 03
    Internal tools need emotional design too. Enterprise software often gets treated as "functional first, aesthetic second." But employees spend hours a day in these interfaces. The blue-tinted surface, the pink accent, the careful hierarchy these reduced resistance to the new tool in ways usability testing couldn't have predicted.
  • 04
    Scope creep reveals real pain, not greed. Billing started as out-of-scope. Bug reporting started as out-of-scope. Both made it into the platform because research kept pointing to them. Learning to say "yes, but in phase 2" while keeping phase 1 intact was one of the harder skills this project demanded.
